name: Set Over Set
context: Villain wins with a top set.

name: Flush Over Flush
context: Villain wins with a King-high flush.

name: Two Pair vs. Straight
context: Villain wins with Two Pair.

name: Missed Draw Bluff
context: Villain wins with a missed flush draw (King-high).

name: Straight vs. Full House
context: Villain wins with a Ten-high straight.

name: Top Pair vs. Second Pair
context: Villain wins with second pair (Jack-kicker).

name: Slowplayed Quads
context: Hero slowplays quad 7s on a paired board, letting the villain bet into them with top pair (Ace-King). Hero wins at showdown.

name: Overpair vs. Top Pair
context: Villain wins with top pair (Ace-kicker).

name: Turned Two Pair
context: Villain wins with an underpair (pocket eights).

name: Rivered Boat
context: Villain wins with a Full House (Tens full of Kings).

name: Phil Ivey vs Eric Persson
context: Hustler Casino Live, 2022. Phil Ivey holds the nut flush draw on the flop while Eric Persson flops top pair with his own flush draw. The river completes Persson's flush to win a massive pot.

name: Tom Dwan vs Wesley Fei
context: Hustler Casino Live, 2023. The largest pot in televised poker history — $3.1 million. Tom Dwan's pocket Queens hold as an overpair against Wesley Fei's Ace-King, which completely misses the board.

name: Robbi Lew vs Garrett Adelstein
context: Hustler Casino Live, 2022. One of the most controversial hands in poker history. Garrett Adelstein moves all-in with a flush draw on a paired board. Robbi Lew makes a stunning call with just a Jack-high — and wins with a better kicker on the shared board.

name: Johnny Chan vs Erik Seidel
context: 1988 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Johnny Chan, defending champion, traps Erik Seidel with a flopped straight on a Queen-high board. Seidel bets his top pair into Chan's disguised nuts. Immortalized in the movie Rounders.

name: Phil Hellmuth vs Johnny Chan
context: 1989 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. 23-year-old Phil Hellmuth becomes the youngest Main Event champion. Hellmuth's pocket nines hold up against defending champion Johnny Chan's Ace-Seven, which picks up a backdoor spade draw but never catches.

name: Mansour Matloubi vs Hans Lund
context: 1990 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Mansour Matloubi calls with pocket Tens against Hans Lund's Ace-Nine. Lund turns two pair but Matloubi rivers a set to win his championship in dramatic fashion.

name: Hamid Dastmalchi vs Tom Jacobs
context: 1992 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Hamid Dastmalchi holds 8-4 offsuit and spikes a straight on the river against Tom Jacobs' flopped two pair. A classic rags-to-riches champion hand.

name: Dan Harrington vs Howard Goldfarb
context: 1995 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. 'Action Dan' Harrington wins with 9-8 against Howard Goldfarb's Ace-Seven. Harrington flops a pair of eights to make two pair on a Queen-high board and never looks back.

name: Huckleberry Seed vs Bruce Van Horn
context: 1996 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Huck Seed flops two pair with 9-8 to defeat Bruce Van Horn's King-Eight. Van Horn picks up a flush draw on the turn but the river bricks and Seed takes the bracelet.

name: Scotty Nguyen vs Kevin McBride
context: 1998 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. With the board pairing to give both players a full house, Scotty Nguyen holds the better full house with a nine in his hand. Before McBride calls the river, Nguyen says "You call, it's gonna be all over, baby" — and he was right.

name: Noel Furlong vs Alan Goehring
context: 1999 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Noel Furlong flops a set of Fives on a Queen-high board, making a full house against Alan Goehring's pocket Sixes. Furlong traps Goehring's two pair all the way to the river.

name: Chris Ferguson vs TJ Cloutier
context: 2000 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. TJ Cloutier holds Ace-Queen and flops top pair, but Chris 'Jesus' Ferguson rivers a nine to make two pair Kings and Nines — cracking Cloutier's pair of Kings and denying him a second bracelet.

name: Carlos Mortensen vs Dewey Tomko
context: 2001 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Carlos Mortensen holds King-Queen and rivers a King-high straight to beat Dewey Tomko's pocket Aces. One of the greatest bad-beat finishes in WSOP championship history.

name: Robert Varkonyi vs Julian Gardner
context: 2002 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Robert Varkonyi flops top pair with Queen-Ten and makes two pair on the river, holding off Julian Gardner's flush draw that never completes. Varkonyi had famously predicted his own victory.

name: John Cynn vs Tony Miles
context: 2018 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. John Cynn holds King-Jack and flops trip Kings to defeat Tony Miles' Queen-Eight. Miles turns two pair but Cynn's three-of-a-kind is dominant. Cynn wins a 12-hour heads-up battle.

name: Stu Ungar vs Mel Judah
context: 1997 WSOP Main Event. Three-time champion Stu Ungar returns after a long absence to win his final championship. Ungar flops top pair of Jacks against Mel Judah's ten-nine, and his pair holds through the river.

name: Stu Ungar vs John Strzemp
context: 1997 WSOP Main Event, earlier heads-up play. Stu Ungar holds Ace-Four and makes a wheel straight on the river against Strzemp's Ace-Eight. Both flop top pair but Ungar's straight is the decisive blow.

name: Brad Daugherty vs Don Holt
context: 1991 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Brad Daugherty wins with a flopped pair of Jacks against Don Holt's seven-three. Daugherty rivers two pair to seal the championship.

name: Jim Bechtel vs Glen Cozen
context: 1993 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Jim Bechtel wins with Jack-high in one of the most underwhelming final hands in championship history. Both players completely miss the board, and Bechtel's Jack edges out Cozen's Ten.

name: Russ Hamilton vs Hugh Vincent
context: 1994 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Russ Hamilton holds 8-5 and flops top pair of Eights. He rivers two pair Eights and Fives to beat Hugh Vincent's King-Eight, which only makes a single pair.

name: Bill Smith vs TJ Cloutier
context: 1985 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Bill Smith wins his first and only bracelet, defeating TJ Cloutier with a pair of Threes against Cloutier's Ace-high. Smith's made hand holds as Cloutier bricks the river.

name: Johnny Chan vs Bob Ciaffone
context: 1987 WSOP Main Event. Johnny Chan wins back-to-back Main Event championships. Here he flops top pair of Kings against Ciaffone's Ace-Four, and holds through the river to take a commanding chip lead.

name: Johnny Chan vs Frank Henderson
context: 1987 WSOP Main Event. Johnny Chan holds Ace-Nine against Henderson's pocket Fours. Chan rivers a pair of Nines to outdraw Henderson's pair and clinch the hand on his way to the championship.

name: Greg Raymer vs David Williams
context: 2004 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Greg 'Fossilman' Raymer holds pocket Eights and flops a set on a board that pairs twice more, giving him a full house by the river. Williams' Ace-Four never finds a hand.

name: Joe Hachem vs Steve Dannenmann
context: 2005 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Joe Hachem holds 7-3 and flops an open-ended straight draw on a 4-5-6 board. The Ace on the turn gives Dannenmann two pair but Hachem's straight is the winner.

name: Jamie Gold vs Paul Wasicka
context: 2006 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Jamie Gold wins the largest first-place prize in poker history at $12 million. Gold's Queen-Nine flops top pair and holds through the river against Wasicka's pocket Tens.

name: Jerry Yang vs Tuan Lam
context: 2007 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Jerry Yang's pocket Eights catch running cards to make a 5-6-7-8-9 straight against Tuan Lam's Ace-Queen, which had flopped top pair. Yang wins against the odds.

name: Peter Eastgate vs Ivan Demidov
context: 2008 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Peter Eastgate becomes the youngest Main Event champion. His Ace-Five makes a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5) on the river against Demidov's four-deuce, which had turned two pair.

name: Joe Cada vs Darvin Moon
context: 2009 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Joe Cada, the youngest Main Event champion, holds pocket Nines against Darvin Moon's King-Queen. Cada rivers a set of Nines to win when Moon's overcards never pair.

name: Jonathan Duhamel vs John Racener
context: 2010 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Jonathan Duhamel holds Ace-Jack and flops a pair of Jacks, then rivers two pair to beat John Racener's King-Eight, which never connects with the board.

name: Pius Heinz vs Martin Staszko
context: 2011 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Pius Heinz holds Ace-King against Staszko's Ten-Seven. Both players completely miss the board and the hand is decided by Heinz's Ace-high over Staszko's Ten-high in a fitting anticlimactic end.

name: Greg Merson vs Jesse Sylvia
context: 2012 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Greg Merson holds King-Five and wins with a pair of Sixes from the board and a King kicker, edging out Jesse Sylvia's Queen kicker in a battle decided by a single card.

name: Ryan Riess vs Jay Farber
context: 2013 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Ryan Riess holds Ace-King and wins with a pair of Fours from the board plus an Ace kicker against Jay Farber's Queen-Five. Riess's commanding chip lead proves too much.

name: Martin Jacobson vs Felix Stephensen
context: 2014 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Martin Jacobson flops a set of Tens to clinch his championship against Felix Stephensen's Ace-Nine. Stephensen turns two pair but Jacobson's trips dominate throughout.

name: Joe McKeehen vs Joshua Beckley
context: 2015 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Joe McKeehen holds Ace-Ten and flops top pair, then turns two pair Aces and Tens to completely dominate Beckley's pocket Fours. McKeehen's chip lead was commanding.

name: Qui Nguyen vs Gordon Vayo
context: 2016 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Qui Nguyen holds King-Ten and flops top pair of Kings, holding off Gordon Vayo's Jack-Ten which never improves. Nguyen's aggressive style powered him to the championship.

name: Scott Blumstein vs Dan Ott
context: 2017 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Scott Blumstein rivers a pair of Deuces to defeat Dan Ott's Ace-Eight. Ott's ace-high leads to the river but Blumstein's deuce spikes to give him the bracelet in a dramatic finish.

name: Doyle Brunson vs Jesse Alto
context: 1976 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Doyle Brunson holds 10-2 and falls behind badly — Alto flops two pair with Ace-Jack and leads on every street. But Brunson spikes runner-runner tens on the turn and river to make Tens full of Twos, winning his first championship bracelet.

name: Doyle Brunson vs Gary Berland
context: 1977 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Impossibly, Doyle Brunson holds 10-2 again and repeats his miracle from the prior year. Berland flops two pair with 8-5 but Brunson hits runner-runner Tens on turn and river to make another Tens full of Twos — back-to-back champions with the same hand.

name: Chris Moneymaker vs Sam Farha
context: 2003 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. The hand that started the poker boom. Amateur Chris Moneymaker holds 5-4 and flops two pair against Sam Farha's Jack-Ten. Farha takes the lead with top pair, but Moneymaker rivers a full house — Fives full of Fours — to become the unlikely world champion.

name: Hossein Ensan vs Dario Sammartino
context: 2019 WSOP Main Event Final Hand, the 50th anniversary WSOP. Hossein Ensan holds pocket Kings and stays in control throughout. Sammartino check-raises all-in with a spade flush draw on the turn, but the draw bricks on the river and Ensan wins his $10 million prize.

name: Koray Aldemir vs George Holmes
context: 2021 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Koray Aldemir flops top two pair with Ten-Seven and slow plays it to the river. George Holmes, with top pair Kings, moves all-in on the river and Aldemir snap-calls to win the $8 million first prize.

name: Espen Jorstad vs Adrian Attenborough
context: 2022 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Espen Jorstad limps with Queen-Deuce and flops trip Twos on a 4-2-2 board. He traps Attenborough's pair of Fours all the way to the river, where a Queen gives Jorstad a full house. Attenborough tanks for nearly 10 minutes before making the losing call.

name: Chanracy Khun vs Doug Polk
context: 2023 WSOP $25,000 Heads-Up Championship Final. Chanracy Khun defeats two-time champion Doug Polk to claim his first WSOP gold bracelet. Khun calls down with Ten-Four and rivers a flush to beat Polk's Ace-Ten, which was ahead the entire way until the final card.

name: Daniel Weinman vs Steven Jones
context: 2023 WSOP Main Event Final Hand. Daniel Weinman holds King-Jack and flops top pair with the superior kicker over Steven Jones's Jack-Eight. Jones shoves all-in with top pair on the turn but Weinman's King kicker dominates to win the record $12.1 million prize.
